text: Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) are widespread, persistent and known to have harmful health and environmental effects at ultratrace levels (ng·L−1). As a result, it is not currently feasible to monitor distributed water systems in real time in the field. Herein, we present amplifying fluorescent polymers (AFPs) for the sensitive, selective detection of the well-known PFAS contaminants, perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) and perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) at the parts-per-trillion level. These AFPs consist of poly(p-phenylene ethynylene) and polyfluorene backbones functionalized with fluorinated domains and pyridine-based PFAS-selectors. The fluorinated domains enhance the hydrophobic interactions between the hydrophobic PFAS analytes and the polymer. Upon binding to the pendant pyridine selectors of the AFP, the protonated PFOA and PFOS undergo a proton-transfer reaction. The analyte--selector interaction produces an energy trap in the polymer excited-state leading to an emission shift to the red end of the spectrum. Our AFPs, as thin films and nanoparticles, exhibit detection limits of 1 ppb and 100 ppt, respectively. Moreover, the robustness of AFPs toward water matrix interference is confirmed via tests with samples of Milli-Q, deionized and well water. These results indicate the suitability of these AFPs for a field applicable, point-of-use (POU) platform that is able to quickly monitor PFAS contamination in the environment at environmentally relevant concentrations.
explanation: The text is highly technical and reads like a scientific abstract with precise measurements. While AI can generate such content, there are no clear stylistic markers that strongly indicate AI authorship, so the probability is moderate.
